loop_cards.who_has: the skill of breaking a problem into an ordered sequence of smaller sub-problems?
planning before coding: identifying inputs, outputs, preconditions and reusable parts?
a condition that must be true before a routine can run correctly?
storing expensive or frequently used results in fast storage for reuse?
cached data that is now out of date because the source has changed?
a self-contained, generalised module that can be used in many programs?
the skill of identifying where the flow of control branches on a condition?
a place in a program where execution branches, e.g. an IF or CASE?
the order in which statements execute, shaped by those branches?
the skill of spotting parts that can run at the same time?
a fault where the result depends on the unpredictable timing of shared tasks?
when tasks each wait for a resource the other holds, so none proceed?
multiple tasks running literally simultaneously on multiple cores?
abstraction that removes detail from one specific thing to model that problem?
abstraction that groups many things by shared characteristics into a general model?
removing or hiding unnecessary detail so only relevant information remains?

apply_cards.example: Give one decision point with an exact Boolean and both branches. IF move is legal -> TRUE: update the board and switch player; FALSE: reject the move and ask again.
Explain one use of caching plus a trade-off. Cache the latest forecast so repeated opens load instantly and cut API calls; trade-off: it can go stale if the forecast updates, so refresh on a timer.
Name two tasks that can run at once and one trade-off. Track the car's GPS while the UI updates the map; trade-off: harder to debug and risk of inconsistent state needing synchronisation.
State two inputs and one output. Inputs - scanned barcode, payment; Output - receipt / payment accepted message.
Name one detail kept and one removed, with reason. Keep road connections and distances; remove the colour of the buildings - irrelevant to finding the shortest route.
Name three ordered sub-procedures. 1) Read teacher/room availability; 2) Assign classes to free slots; 3) Output the finished timetable.
State a precondition that must hold before dispensing cash. The account balance must be at least the requested amount (and the PIN already verified).
Give one reusable component and a benefit. A blur filter module reused by many tools - faster development and fewer bugs as it is already tested.
Identify a risk of letting players answer simultaneously. A race condition when two answers update the shared scoreboard at once - needs locking/synchronisation; not automatically faster.
Give one decision point with an exact Boolean and both branches. IF stockLevel >= quantityOrdered -> TRUE: confirm the order and reduce stock; FALSE: show out of stock.

quickfire.clue: I removed the room's carpet colour from my model because it doesn't affect bookings.
I worked out the inputs, outputs and what must be true first, before writing any code.
I split 'make a booking' into check, store and confirm steps in a fixed order.
I wrote IF seatsRequested <= seatsAvailable and planned both branches.
I spotted that logging and rendering the screen have no data dependency, so they can overlap.
The list must be sorted before this binary search will work.
I stored the frequently requested results in fast memory to avoid recomputing them.
My cached price list is wrong because the database changed but the cache didn't.
Two students booked the same slot at the same instant and the result depended on timing.
I grouped savings and current accounts into one general Account model.
Each task is waiting for a resource the other is holding, so nothing moves.
I made one tested, self-contained module and used it across three programs.
